label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, so I need to figure out if an 8×8 chessboard with two diagonally opposite corners removed can be completely tiled with 31 dominoes. Each domino covers two adjacent squares, right? Let me start by visualizing the chessboard. Normally, a full chessboard has 64 squares, alternating between black and white. If we remove two diagonally opposite corners, those two squares are both the same color. For example, if you take the top-left and bottom-right corners, both are typically light squares, or if they're dark, depends on how the board is colored. But the key point is that they are the same color. So removing two squares of the same color would leave us with 32 squares of one color and 30 of the other. Now, each domino, when placed on the board, will always cover one black and one white square, no matter how you place it—horizontally or vertically. Because adjacent squares are always opposite colors. Therefore, each domino covers one of each. If we have 31 dominoes, that would mean they cover 31 black and 31 white squares. But the board now has 32 of one color and 30 of the other. So there's an imbalance here. 31 and 31 doesn't add up to 62, but wait, 31 dominoes would cover 62 squares. The total remaining squares are 62. But the color count is 32 and 30. So if you try to cover them with dominoes, each domino takes one of each color, so you can only have a maximum of 30 dominoes if you have 30 pairs, but then there would be two squares left of the same color, which can't be covered by a domino. Therefore, it's impossible. So the answer should be no. But wait, let me check again. Let me think if there's another way. Suppose the dominoes can somehow... but no, each domino must cover one black and one white. Therefore, the total number of black and white squares needs to be equal. Since removing two corners of the same color makes them unequal, it's impossible. Is there a different tiling strategy? Maybe if the dominoes are placed in some non-adjacent way? No, dominoes must cover two adjacent squares. Since adjacency ensures opposite colors.
